1.synthesis:
The divine response to the lament-prayer of chapters 63–64 begins not with comfort but with a reversal: it was not YHWH who was absent but Israel who was unreachable. “I was ready to be sought by those who did not ask for me; I was ready to be found by those who did not seek me. I said, ‘Here I am, here I am,’ to a nation that was not called by my name” (Isa 65:1). The double hinneni hinneni (Here I am! Here I am!) reverses the standard posture of the prophetic call: usually the prophet cries “here I am” to the divine summons; here YHWH calls and Israel ignores. He stretched out his hands throughout the day to a rebellious people walking in the way that is not good (v. 2): the parental posture of patient outstretched welcome meeting the “rebellious son” of Deuteronomy 21:18. Paul reads verses 1–2 in Romans 10:20–21 as the textual warrant for the Gentile mission and Israel’s resistance.

The specific indictments of verses 3–5 catalog the syncretistic practices that competed with orthodox Yahwism in the exilic and post-exilic period: sacrificing in gardens (outdoor fertility-cult shrines), burning incense on bricks (a Babylonian building material—suggesting practices borrowed during the exile), sitting in tombs to receive revelatory dreams from the dead (necromancy), eating pig’s flesh and unclean broth, and the most ironic offense of all: saying “Keep to yourself, do not come near me, for I am holy to you” (v. 5)—appropriating the Holiness Code’s language of YHWH’s exclusive holiness to protect their own forbidden practices. Those who defile themselves most thoroughly claim the greatest purity. YHWH’s record-keeping is declared: “it is written before me; I will not keep silent but I will repay” (v. 6)—answering the lament’s closing challenge (“will you keep silent?”) with unexpected directness.

The grape-cluster parable of verse 8 introduces the remnant theology that will govern the rest of the chapter: as new wine is found within an otherwise spoiled cluster (“do not destroy it, for there is a blessing in it”), YHWH will preserve a seed from Jacob, a remnant who will inherit his mountains (v. 9). The double-destiny discourse of verses 13–16 structures the bifurcation as a series of antitheses—my servants shall eat but you shall be hungry; my servants shall rejoice but you shall be ashamed; my servants shall sing but you shall cry out in pain of heart (vv. 13–14). The rebellious community’s name becomes a curse-formula for the faithful; YHWH’s servants receive a new name (v. 15). The section closes with the “God of Amen/Truth” formula (v. 16)—the Elohei Amen—and the announcement that the former troubles are forgotten, preparing for the new creation oracle that follows.
1.historical_context: The garden-cult practices and tomb-sleeping of verses 3–4 reflect the syncretistic popular religion of the post-exilic period, which Malachi and Nehemiah also confront. The Gad and Meni deities of verse 11 are ANE fate-deities (Gad = fortune; Meni = destiny), whose worship addressed the existential anxiety of a community whose survival felt contingent on cosmic powers. The wordplay on meni (Destiny) and YHWH’s maniti (I will destine you) in verse 12 is one of the sharpest polemical wordplays in the chapter: you chose the deity of Destiny, so YHWH himself will appoint your destiny—to the sword. Paul’s application of verses 1–2 in Romans 10:20–21 establishes that the Gentile receptivity to the gospel and Israel’s resistance are not accidents but the fulfillment of Isaiah’s prophetic typology.
1.literary_structure: The Judgment-on-the-Rebellious section (vv. 1–16) moves from YHWH’s complaint about Israel’s non-response to his calling (vv. 1–2) through the indictment of specific practices (vv. 3–7) through the grape-cluster remnant principle (v. 8) through the remnant’s promise (vv. 9–10) through the fate-deity worship condemned (vv. 11–12) to the double-destiny discourse (vv. 13–16). The grape-cluster parable is the structural pivot: it establishes the principle that judgment and preservation are not contradictory but simultaneous operations within the same community.
1.theological_application: The divine outstretched hands of verse 2—YHWH holding his arms out all day to a rebellious people—is one of the most poignant images of prevenient grace in the Hebrew Bible: divine initiative sustained over long periods against persistent human resistance. The grape-cluster principle (v. 8) establishes that the presence of a remnant of faithful within a community alters YHWH’s response to the whole: “do not destroy it, for there is a blessing in it.” Communities shaped by this passage develop a theology of the faithful remnant as preserving the whole: the ten righteous who could have saved Sodom (Gen 18:32) are the structural analogy. The double-destiny discourse warns that proximity to the covenant community and even formal religious observance does not guarantee the servant-outcome: the same community, the same religious forms, the same historical situation produce opposite trajectories for those who trust YHWH and those who have substituted fate-deities for him.
1.christology: Paul’s citation of Isaiah 65:1 in Romans 10:20 (“I was found by those who did not seek me; I revealed myself to those who did not ask for me”) and of verse 2 in Romans 10:21 (“all day long I have stretched out my hands to a disobedient and obstinate people”) establishes the structural reading of the Gentile mission: the nations who were not seeking YHWH found him in Christ; Israel, who had the covenant tradition, has largely rejected him. The double-destiny discourse of verses 13–14 anticipates the judgment parables of Jesus: the same banquet, the same master, the same invitation produces opposite outcomes for those who respond and those who do not (Matt 22:1–14; Luke 14:15–24).

17.synthesis:
The great new-creation oracle is the climax toward which the entire final section of Isaiah has been building: “For behold, I create new heavens and a new earth; the former things shall not be remembered or come to mind” (Isa 65:17). The verb bor’e (creating) is the same exclusive divine-creation term as Genesis 1:1: the new creation is not renovation but a creative act of the same order as the original. The forgetting of the former world is not amnesia but the eclipse of the old by the overwhelming goodness of the new—a gracious mercy that spares the restored community from perpetual grief over what was lost.

The new creation is anchored to a particular location: “Behold, I create Jerusalem to be a joy and her people to be a gladness” (v. 18). The cosmic new creation is not an abstraction but the transformation of a specific covenantal city. The mutual joy—YHWH rejoicing in Jerusalem and her people (v. 19)—establishes the relational content: the new creation is defined by the joy of restored covenant intimacy, and its audible marker is the absence of weeping. The altered relationship to death is intermediate—not the full abolition of death (that belongs to Isaiah 25:8 and Revelation 21:4) but radical life-extension: a hundred-year-old person will be considered a youth (v. 20).

The covenant-curse reversals of verses 21–23 precisely invert the Deuteronomy 28 curse-structure: those who build houses will inhabit them and those who plant vineyards will eat their fruit (reversing Deut 28:30—“you shall build a house but not dwell in it; you shall plant a vineyard but not enjoy its fruit”). The blessing extends generationally: “they and their offspring with them” (v. 23). The relational intimacy of verse 24—“before they call I will answer; while they are yet speaking I will hear”—ends the silence-complaint that has haunted the lament (chapters 63–64): the hiddenness is over. The oracle closes with the peaceable kingdom image that Isaiah 11:6–9 introduced: wolf and lamb grazing together, lion eating straw like an ox, serpent eating dust as per Genesis 3:14. The Eden-created harmony between creatures is restored; only the serpent’s curse remains, the one element from the old order that memorializes the judgment that was escaped.
17.historical_context: The verb bor’e (create) in verse 17 appears exclusively in divine-creation contexts throughout the Hebrew Bible; its use here ensures that the new-creation oracle carries the full weight of cosmological newness rather than mere restoration. The specific reversal of Deuteronomy 28:30 curse-language in verses 21–22 demonstrates Isaiah’s structural inversion of the Mosaic covenant-curse framework: every element of the curse list is now eliminated in the new creation. Revelation 21:1 and 2 Peter 3:13 both draw directly on the new-heavens-and-new-earth language of verse 17. The before-they-call-I-will-answer promise of verse 24 directly answers the silence-complaint of the lament (Isa 64:12): the new creation is defined by the elimination of the communication breakdown that exile represented.
17.literary_structure: The New-Creation section (vv. 17–25) moves from the cosmic announcement (v. 17) to the particular Zion-centered joy (vv. 18–19) to the altered death-relationship (v. 20) to the covenant-curse reversals (vv. 21–23) to the before-they-call responsiveness (v. 24) to the peaceable kingdom (v. 25). The movement is from cosmic to personal to social to relational to ecological: the new creation encompasses every dimension of the old creation’s fractures. The serpent-eating-dust of verse 25 is the single element from Genesis 3 that is not reversed but preserved, creating a permanent asymmetry: the new creation is new, but it carries the memory of what it costs.
17.theological_application: The before-they-call-I-will-answer promise (v. 24) describes the fullness of covenant intimacy: a communication so complete that YHWH is already responding as the prayer is being formed. This is not merely faster prayer-answering but a transformation of the relationship itself—from the distance and silence of exile to the immediacy of full covenant presence. Communities shaped by this passage hold the new-creation hope not as an escape from history but as the transformation of history: the specific city, the specific people, the specific covenant curses are all taken up and reversed. The peaceable kingdom of verse 25—identical to Isaiah 11:6–9—confirms that the messianic governance and the new creation are the same eschatological reality from two angles.
17.christology: Paul’s new-creation language in 2 Corinthians 5:17 (“if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; the old has passed away, the new has come”) and Galatians 6:15 (“neither circumcision counts for anything, nor uncircumcision, but a new creation”) draws on the Isaiah 65:17 framework: the new creation is the condition of those who are in Christ. The before-they-call-I-will-answer promise (v. 24) is the Old Testament ground for Jesus’s prayer promises in John 16:23–24: “Ask and you will receive, that your joy may be full.” Revelation 21:1 (“then I saw a new heaven and a new earth”) and Revelation 21:4 (“he will wipe away every tear; death shall be no more”) take the Isaiah 65 vision to its final completion in the new Jerusalem, where the intermediate stage—extended life but still mortal—is superseded by the full abolition of death in Christ’s resurrection.
